text: So this is a video just specifically of setting, how I set up my isoparastheltic anastomosis. A lot of people use V-Lock, I like to use Vicryl, and I'll show you why in a second. So basically what I do is I set up these Vicryl stay sutures on either side of my future enterotomy.
The surgeon ties a square knot, converts it to a slip knot, and then back to a square knot, ensuring the correct tension on each stitch. The tail of the suture is left intact for future use.
The surgeon estimates approximately twelve millimeters from the last stitch to position two stay sutures, which will control the enterotomies and facilitate the insertion of the stapler.
The surgeon creates enterotomies while controlling both edges with previously placed stitches, and then inserts the stapler into the surgical site.
The surgeon uses two Vicryl stay sutures to dynamically guide each corner of the tissue onto the stapler, maintaining control over the staple anastomosis process.

What I like here is that I see both ends of the staple line. And we can control that with this move right here. So you come in, outside the end, and then you grab that staple line, hook it, and then dunk it under. And you know that you got that corner. That's what I like.
And so then I'm going to talk about a couple of the techniques I use. So I lift up and I push down right in front of my suture.
The surgeon uses a needle tip to set the suture, taking full thickness bites through the tissue. The staple line is pushed in and dumped down to secure the area.
The surgeon advances beyond the last stitch, tying the suture to the previous suture's tie, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon cuts both ends of the sutures and performs a Lembert stitch, followed by a running Lembert stitch below. The process involves guiding the residents through the technique.

The surgeon sets the suture using the needle, adjusting tension by pushing with the needle's back. The suture is placed beyond the stay, with one stitch in front and one behind, ensuring proper tension throughout the process.
